lessons.title: Horizontal Angle Measurement
learning_objectives: Define a horizontal angle and distinguish interior, exterior, deflection, and angle-to-the-right observations.
Describe how a total station measures a horizontal angle as the difference of two circle readings.
Explain the direct-and-reverse (face left / face right) procedure and which instrument errors it cancels.
Apply the method of repetition and the method of directions (sets) to improve angular precision.
key_takeaways: A horizontal angle is the difference between two directions measured in a horizontal plane, independent of the points' elevations.
Observing an angle in both faces (direct and reverse) and meaning the two cancels collimation, trunnion-axis, and circle-eccentricity errors.
The method of repetition and the method of directions (sets) reduce random error by averaging multiple independent pointings.
Always record a backsight, a foresight, and the resulting angle; an angle with no independent check cannot be trusted.

content.html:
What a Horizontal Angle Is

A horizontal angle is the angle, measured in a horizontal plane, between two directions radiating from an occupied point. It is the second of the surveyor's two great families of field measurement — distances locate how far, angles locate which way. Together they let you carry direction and position from a known point out to new ones, which is the entire basis of traversing.

The key word is horizontal. Even when the backsight target and the foresight target sit at very different elevations, the instrument's graduated horizontal circle reads only the projection of those directions onto a level plane. A modern total station levels precisely over the occupied point and reports the horizontal angle directly, regardless of the up-and-down geometry. This independence from elevation is exactly what makes the horizontal angle the natural quantity for plan-view position and bearing.

How the Instrument Measures It

A total station does not measure an angle in a single reading; it measures two directions and takes their difference. You sight the backsight and note (or zero) the horizontal-circle reading, then sight the foresight and read the circle again. The horizontal angle is simply:

  • Angle = (foresight reading) − (backsight reading), carried clockwise and adding 360° if the result comes out negative.

Field conventions classify the angle by how it is turned. An angle to the right is measured clockwise from backsight to foresight and is the modern default for traverse work. An interior angle lies inside a closed figure; an exterior angle outside it; a deflection angle is measured from the prolongation of the previous line, right (R) or left (L). Whatever the convention, the discipline is the same: record both the backsight and the foresight so the angle can be reconstructed and checked.

content.alt: Plan-view diagram of an occupied station with a backsight and foresight, showing the clockwise horizontal angle to the right between them.
content.caption: Figure: A horizontal angle is the difference between the foresight and backsight directions, measured clockwise (angle to the right).

content.html:
Direct and Reverse (Two Faces)

No instrument is perfectly adjusted. The line of sight may not be exactly perpendicular to the horizontal (trunnion) axis (collimation error); the trunnion axis may not be exactly horizontal; the graduated circle may be slightly eccentric to the vertical axis. The classic, powerful remedy is to observe every angle in both faces: once in the direct position (face left), then plunge the telescope and rotate 180° to observe in the reverse position (face right). Because these instrument errors reverse sign between the two faces, meaning the direct and reverse results cancels them.

A complete observation of one angle in both faces is one set (or position, or D&R pair). The mean of the two faces also serves as an immediate field check: if the face-left and face-right angles disagree by more than a few seconds (for the instrument's grade), something is wrong — a mis-pointing, a bubble out of level, or a blunder — and you re-observe before leaving the station.

content.text: Double-centering (observing both faces and meaning the results) cancels collimation, trunnion-axis, and circle-eccentricity errors. These are systematic errors, so repetition alone cannot remove them — you must reverse the instrument. Random pointing error, by contrast, is what additional sets average down.

content.html:
Repetition and Directions (Sets)

To beat down random pointing and reading error, surveyors take multiple observations and average. Two classic schemes:

  • Method of repetition. With a repeating instrument the same angle is accumulated several times on the circle without re-zeroing; the total is divided by the number of repetitions. Accumulating the angle effectively multiplies it, so the small circle-reading error is divided by the number of reps, improving the result.
  • Method of directions (sets). With a modern directional total station you observe each target's direction (D and R), record the directions, and reduce angles as differences of meaned directions. Several such sets are observed and their angles averaged; the standard error of the mean improves as √n.

The number of sets is matched to the required precision: a routine boundary traverse might use two sets, while precise control or a property of high value may demand four or more. The governing statistics are the same error-of-the-mean rules from the measurements module — each independent set is another observation that drives the random component down by √n.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: A horizontal angle is the difference of two directions in a horizontal plane and is independent of target elevation.
The instrument measures two circle readings; the angle is foresight minus backsight, taken clockwise for an angle to the right.
Observing both faces (direct and reverse) cancels collimation, trunnion-axis, and circle-eccentricity errors and provides a field check.
Repetition and multiple sets average down random pointing/reading error, improving precision by sqrt(n).

flashcards.definition: The angle between two directions measured in a horizontal plane from an occupied point. It is computed as the difference between the foresight and backsight circle readings and is independent of the targets' elevations.
A horizontal angle measured clockwise from the backsight to the foresight. It is the standard convention for modern traverse observations.

Observing an angle once in the direct (face left) position and once in the reverse (face right) position and meaning the results, which cancels collimation, trunnion-axis, and circle-eccentricity errors and provides a field check.
Accumulating the same angle several times on the circle without re-zeroing, then dividing by the number of repetitions, so that the circle-reading error is reduced and random error is averaged down.

lessons.title: Vertical Angle Measurement
learning_objectives: Distinguish a vertical (altitude) angle measured from horizontal from a zenith angle measured from the vertical, and convert between them.
Explain the sign convention for vertical angles (plus above horizontal, minus below).
Describe how observing both faces removes the vertical-circle index error.
Apply vertical and zenith angles to reduce a slope distance to horizontal and vertical components.
key_takeaways: A vertical angle is measured up or down from the horizontal (0 deg at horizontal); a zenith angle is measured down from straight up (0 deg at the zenith).
Zenith angle = 90 deg - vertical angle; equivalently vertical angle = 90 deg - zenith angle.
Meaning the face-left and face-right vertical readings removes the vertical-circle index error.
Vertical/zenith angles convert a measured slope distance into horizontal distance and elevation difference.

content.html:
Two Ways to Reference the Same Direction

Where a horizontal angle answers "which way," a vertical angle answers "how steep." It is the angle of a line of sight measured in a vertical plane. There are two competing references, and confusing them is a common student error. A vertical (altitude) angle is measured from the horizontal: 0° when the telescope is level, positive when sighting above horizontal, negative when sighting below. A zenith angle is measured from the zenith (straight up): 0° pointing at the sky directly overhead, 90° when the telescope is level, and more than 90° when depressed below horizontal.

Most modern total stations read the zenith angle by default, because a zenith circle has no sign ambiguity — every sight produces a single positive number between 0° and 180°. Field notes and older instruments, however, frequently use the horizontal-referenced vertical angle with its plus/minus sign. You must know which your instrument reports and be fluent converting between them.

content.label: Zenith angle Z and vertical (altitude) angle V

content.html:
The Conversion in Practice

The relationship is straightforward: a level sight is 0° vertical and 90° zenith, so the two always sum to 90°. A sight elevated 6° above horizontal is a vertical angle of +6° and a zenith angle of 84°. A sight depressed 6° below horizontal is −6° vertical and 96° zenith. Because the zenith value is always positive, it travels through trigonometric reduction formulas cleanly — you do not have to track a sign separately.

Two practical cautions. First, the angle you measure is to the target (prism or rod reading), not the ground; reducing to a point on the ground requires accounting for instrument height and target height. Second, over long sights the curvature of the earth and atmospheric refraction bend the apparent vertical angle slightly; precise trigonometric heighting applies a combined curvature-and-refraction correction, a topic developed fully in the leveling module.

content.text: Do not mix references. A total station reading of 91 deg 30 min is a ZENITH angle (1 deg 30 min below horizontal), NOT a 91.5 deg vertical angle. Always confirm whether your instrument and field notes use zenith (0 deg up, 90 deg level) or vertical/altitude (0 deg level, signed).

content.html:
Index Error and the Two-Face Cure

The vertical circle has its own systematic flaw, the vertical-circle index error: the reading when the line of sight is truly horizontal may differ from the ideal (0° vertical / 90° zenith) by a small constant. Left uncorrected it biases every vertical or zenith angle by the same amount.

The cure mirrors the horizontal case: observe the target in both faces. In the direct and reversed positions the index error enters with opposite sign, so a correct reduction of the two faces removes it. For zenith angles a common check is that the face-left and face-right readings should sum to 360°; their departure from 360° is twice the index error. Meaning the two faces (after reducing one to the other's frame) yields an index-free vertical angle. As always, the two-face disagreement is also a real-time blunder check.

Using Vertical Angles: Reducing Slope

The chief reason a total station measures a vertical (zenith) angle along with a slope distance is to split that slope distance into its horizontal and vertical components. If S is the measured slope distance and z the zenith angle, then the horizontal distance is H = S·sin z and the elevation difference is ΔV = S·cos z (plus the instrument-minus-target height term). In the vertical-angle convention with angle v, the same reduction is H = S·cos v and ΔV = S·sin v.

This single pair of formulas is the engine of three-dimensional positioning with a total station: every shot to a prism yields a direction, a horizontal distance, and an elevation change, all from one pointing. The same trigonometry powers trigonometric leveling, where a vertical angle and a distance substitute for a level run to carry elevation across terrain.

content.latex: H = S\,\sin z = S\,\cos v \qquad \Delta V = S\,\cos z = S\,\sin v
content.label: Reducing slope distance S with zenith angle z or vertical angle v

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Vertical (altitude) angles are measured from horizontal (signed); zenith angles are measured from straight up (always positive).
Z = 90 deg - V; a level sight is 0 deg vertical and 90 deg zenith.
Observing both faces removes the vertical-circle index error; face-left + face-right zenith readings should sum to 360 deg.
With slope distance S: H = S*sin(z) = S*cos(v) and elevation change = S*cos(z) = S*sin(v).

flashcards.definition: An angle measured up or down from the horizontal plane: 0 degrees when level, positive above horizontal, negative below.

An angle measured downward from the zenith (straight up): 0 degrees at the zenith, 90 degrees at horizontal, more than 90 degrees below horizontal. Always positive. Related to the vertical angle by Z = 90 deg - V.
A constant systematic offset in the vertical-circle reading at a true horizontal sight. It is removed by observing the target in both instrument faces.

lessons.title: Bearings and Azimuths
learning_objectives: Define azimuth and bearing and state the reference (north) and range of each.
Convert an azimuth to a quadrant bearing and a bearing back to an azimuth in any quadrant.
Compute a back-azimuth (azimuth +/- 180 deg) and a back-bearing (same angle, opposite quadrant).
Recognize how a stated basis of bearing fixes the meaning of every direction in a survey.
key_takeaways: An azimuth is a clockwise angle from north, 0 deg to 360 deg; a bearing is an acute angle (0 deg to 90 deg) from north or south toward east or west.
Back-azimuth = azimuth +/- 180 deg; a back-bearing keeps the same numeric angle but flips both letters (N->S, E->W).
Azimuth-to-bearing conversion depends on the quadrant the azimuth falls in (NE, SE, SW, NW).
Every direction is meaningless without a stated basis of bearing; Texas descriptions must cite that basis.

content.html:
Two Languages for Direction

An angle tells you how directions differ; a direction tells you where a line points relative to a fixed reference, almost always north. Surveyors express direction in two interchangeable languages. An azimuth is a single angle turned clockwise from north, ranging from 0° to 360°. A bearing is an acute angle, 0° to 90°, measured from the nearer meridian end (north or south) toward the east or west, and written with two letters bracketing the angle, e.g. N 35°15′ E or S 72°40′ W.

Both describe the same physical line; they are simply different notations. Azimuths are computational favorites because direction arithmetic (adding deflection angles, computing latitudes and departures) is cleanest with a single 0–360° value. Bearings dominate legal descriptions and plats because the quadrant letters communicate orientation at a glance. A working surveyor converts between them constantly.

Converting Azimuth to Bearing

The conversion is entirely about which quadrant the azimuth falls in. Picture the compass split into four 90° quadrants. If the azimuth is between 0° and 90° (northeast), the bearing is simply N (azimuth) E — for an azimuth of 35°, the bearing is N 35° E. Between 90° and 180° (southeast) the line points south-and-east, so subtract from 180°: an azimuth of 145° becomes S 35° E. Between 180° and 270° (southwest) subtract 180°: 215° becomes S 35° W. Between 270° and 360° (northwest) subtract from 360°: 325° becomes N 35° W.

Going the other way (bearing to azimuth) reverses these rules, as the table shows. The single habit that prevents nearly all errors is to identify the quadrant first — from the azimuth's range, or from the bearing's two letters — and then apply the matching formula. Sketch the line if there is any doubt.

Back-Azimuths and Back-Bearings

Every line has two ends, and the direction looking back along it is the reverse direction. For an azimuth, the back-azimuth is the forward azimuth ± 180°: add 180° if the forward azimuth is less than 180°, subtract 180° if it is 180° or more, so the result stays within 0–360°. A forward azimuth of 70° has a back-azimuth of 250°; a forward azimuth of 250° has a back-azimuth of 70°.

For a bearing, the back-bearing keeps the same numeric angle but flips both letters: north becomes south and east becomes west. The back-bearing of N 35° E is S 35° W; the back-bearing of S 72° W is N 72° E. This reciprocity is constantly used in traverse computation, where the direction into a station (a back-direction) plus the measured angle yields the direction out of it to the next station.

content.text: Texas: a bearing is meaningless without a stated BASIS OF BEARING. The Texas rules for professional surveying (22 TAC 138.91(c)) require every description to reference its basis of bearing — an identifiable monumented line or an established geodetic system such as the Texas State Plane Coordinate System (NAD83) grid. Historic deeds often used magnetic or assumed bearings, a frequent source of rotational discrepancy when retracing them today.

content.html:
Why the Reference Matters

Azimuths and bearings are only as meaningful as the north they are measured from. There are several norths: true (geodetic) north toward the earth's rotational pole, grid north aligned to a coordinate-system projection (such as a State Plane zone), magnetic north toward the magnetic pole, and an arbitrary assumed north. A bearing of N 35° E means a different physical line under each reference.

For this reason a defensible survey always states its basis of bearing: the specific reference to which all directions are tied. Modern Texas practice ties to grid north on the Texas State Plane Coordinate System (NAD83). When you retrace an older description written on magnetic or assumed bearings, you must reconcile its rotated framework to the modern datum — failing to do so plants the whole figure at the wrong orientation even when every internal angle is perfect.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Azimuth: clockwise from north, 0 to 360 deg. Bearing: acute angle from N or S toward E or W, written as a quadrant.
Convert by quadrant: NE A=B; SE 180-A; SW A-180 / 180+B; NW 360-A.
Back-azimuth = azimuth +/- 180 deg; back-bearing keeps the angle and flips both letters.
All directions require a basis of bearing; Texas descriptions must state it, and historic magnetic/assumed bearings must be reconciled to the modern datum.

flashcards.definition: A horizontal direction expressed as a clockwise angle from north, ranging from 0 to 360 degrees. Preferred for computation because direction arithmetic uses a single value.
A horizontal direction expressed as an acute angle (0 to 90 degrees) measured from north or south toward east or west, written with two letters, e.g., N 35 deg 15 min E.
The azimuth of a line viewed from its far end, equal to the forward azimuth plus or minus 180 degrees so that it stays within 0 to 360 degrees.
The reference (true/geodetic north, grid north, magnetic north, or an assumed line) to which all directions in a survey are tied. Texas (22 TAC 138.91(c)) requires it to be stated in the description.

lessons.title: Compass and Magnetic Declination
learning_objectives: Explain how a magnetic compass establishes direction and why magnetic north differs from true north.
Define magnetic declination (east or west) and apply it to convert between magnetic and true bearings.
Account for the secular change of declination when retracing old magnetic-bearing descriptions.
Describe local attraction and how comparing forward and back bearings detects it.
key_takeaways: Magnetic declination is the horizontal angle between true north and magnetic north; east declination is positive, west declination negative.
True bearing = magnetic bearing + east declination (or - west declination); reverse the sign to go from true to magnetic.
Declination changes with time (secular change), so retracing an old magnetic survey requires the declination of the original survey's date and place.
Local attraction from nearby iron or current biases a compass; equal-and-opposite forward/back bearing discrepancies reveal it.

content.html:
The Compass as a Direction Instrument

For two centuries the magnetic compass — on a surveyor's compass or a transit's compass box — was the primary means of establishing direction in the field. A freely pivoting magnetized needle aligns with the horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field and points toward magnetic north. Reading the needle against the graduated circle gives a magnetic bearing directly. Compasses are simple, cheap, and self-orienting, which is why countless original land descriptions — including a great many early Texas field notes — are recorded in magnetic bearings.

The limitation is fundamental: a compass points to the magnetic pole, not the geographic (true) pole, and the magnetic field wanders in both space and time. To use compass directions rigorously — and especially to retrace an old magnetic-bearing survey today — you must understand and correct for the difference between magnetic and true north.

Magnetic Declination

Magnetic declination (the surveyor's term; geographers say magnetic variation) is the horizontal angle at a place between true north and magnetic north. If the needle points east of true north, the declination is east (positive); if west of true north, west (negative). Declination varies by location: across the continental United States it ranges from strongly east in the west to strongly west in the east, passing through an agonic line of zero declination.

The conversion rule follows directly from the geometry. To convert a magnetic bearing to a true bearing, add an east declination and subtract a west declination: true = magnetic + declination, taking east as positive. To go from true back to magnetic, reverse the sign. A clean sketch — true north up, magnetic north offset by the declination — settles the sign every time and is worth drawing whenever you convert.

Declination Changes With Time

Declination is not constant; the earth's magnetic field drifts, so the declination at a given place changes year to year — the secular change. Over the decades or centuries separating an original survey from its retracement, the change can amount to several degrees, which on a long line is a large positional shift. This is the heart of why old magnetic descriptions are treacherous: a bearing of N 12° E recorded in 1860 does not point the same way as N 12° E observed magnetically today.

To retrace a magnetic-bearing survey properly you need the declination of the original survey's place and date, not today's. Surveyors obtain historic declination from models such as NOAA's, and may compute the change between then and now to rotate the old calls into a modern true or grid framework. The professional standard is to re-establish the lines from surviving monuments wherever possible and use the magnetic conversion only to reconstruct direction where monuments are lost — consistent with the rule that monuments outrank course.

content.text: Texas retracement: many original GLO field notes use magnetic or assumed bearings. Because declination has changed since the survey, you cannot simply read the old bearings on a modern instrument. Recover original monuments first (they control over course); use the original-date declination only to reconstruct lost lines, then tie the result to the modern basis of bearing (Texas State Plane, NAD83).

content.html:
Local Attraction

Beyond the regional declination, a compass needle can be deflected locally by nearby iron or steel (a fence, a vehicle, a rebar, a power line, even a belt buckle or eyeglasses) or by local geology. This local attraction biases readings taken at a particular spot but does not affect the angle between two lines observed from the same point.

The classic test is to compare a line's forward and back bearings, read from its two ends. In the absence of local attraction they should differ by exactly 180° (a true reciprocal). If the discrepancy departs from 180°, local attraction is present at one or both stations, and the surveyor isolates the clean station, then carries direction forward using the angles between lines (which are unaffected) rather than the contaminated absolute bearings. This forward/back check is a direct application of the redundancy principle: an independent observation that exposes a systematic local error.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: A compass points to magnetic north; declination is the angle between true and magnetic north (east positive, west negative).
True bearing = magnetic bearing + east declination (subtract a west declination); reverse to go true-to-magnetic.
Declination drifts over time (secular change), so retracing old magnetic surveys needs the original date-and-place declination.
Local attraction biases absolute compass bearings; a forward/back bearing that is not exactly 180 deg apart reveals it.

flashcards.definition: The horizontal angle at a place between true north and magnetic north. East (positive) when the needle points east of true north; west (negative) when it points west.
flashcards.hint_1: Angle between true north and where the needle points.

flashcards.definition: The gradual change in magnetic declination over time as the earth's magnetic field drifts. It forces a retracing surveyor to use the declination of the original survey's date, not today's.

A local deflection of the compass needle caused by nearby iron, steel, current, or magnetic geology. It biases absolute bearings but not the angle between lines; detected when forward and back bearings do not differ by exactly 180 degrees.

lessons.title: Deflection Angles and Interior Angles
learning_objectives: Define interior, exterior, and deflection angles and identify where each is used.
State and apply the geometric condition that the interior angles of a closed n-sided polygon sum to (n-2)*180 deg.
Compute the exterior-angle condition (sum = (n+2)*180 deg) and the deflection-angle condition (algebraic sum = 360 deg).
Use these geometric conditions as an independent check on a set of measured traverse angles.
key_takeaways: Interior angles lie inside a closed figure; exterior angles outside; deflection angles are measured from the prolongation of the previous line, right or left.
For a closed polygon of n sides, the interior angles must sum to (n-2)*180 deg.
The exterior angles of a closed polygon sum to (n+2)*180 deg, and the deflection angles (R positive, L negative) sum algebraically to 360 deg.
These fixed geometric totals are independent checks that expose angular blunders before adjustment.

content.html:
Three Ways to Turn a Traverse Angle

When a traverse changes direction at a station, the turn can be recorded as one of three angle types, and each has its place. An interior angle is measured on the inside of a closed figure, between the two adjacent traverse lines; closed boundary traverses are most often run with interior angles (taken as angles to the right going around the figure). An exterior angle is its companion on the outside; interior plus exterior at any station equals 360°. A deflection angle is measured not between the two lines directly but from the prolongation (straight-ahead extension) of the incoming line to the outgoing line, and is labeled right (R) or left (L) depending on which way the route bends.

Deflection angles shine on open, route-type traverses — highways, pipelines, transmission lines — where the natural question at each point is "how far off straight do we turn, and which way?" Interior angles shine on closed property traverses, where the figure must close on itself. Knowing which convention is in use is essential to reducing the angles correctly.

content.alt: Diagram contrasting an interior angle inside a closed polygon, its exterior angle outside, and a deflection angle measured from the prolongation of the previous line.
content.caption: Figure: Interior, exterior, and deflection angles at a traverse station. The deflection angle is measured from the dashed prolongation of the incoming line.

content.html:
The Interior-Angle Sum Condition

The single most useful geometric fact in angle work is the angle-sum condition for a closed polygon: the interior angles of any simple closed figure with n sides must total (n − 2) × 180°. A triangle (n = 3) sums to 180°, a quadrilateral (n = 4) to 360°, a pentagon (n = 5) to 540°, and so on — each added side contributes another 180°.

This is not an approximation or a convention; it is a theorem of plane geometry. That makes it a perfect independent check. After turning all the interior angles of a closed traverse, you add them up and compare the measured total to the required (n − 2)×180°. Any difference is the angular misclosure — the accumulated angular error of the loop. A small misclosure (within the expected random spread for your instrument and number of angles) means the angle work is good; a large one signals a blunder to hunt down before going further.

content.label: Interior-angle sum of a closed n-sided polygon

content.html:
Exterior and Deflection Conditions

The same geometry gives companion checks for the other angle types. Because interior and exterior angles at each of the n stations sum to 360°, the exterior angles of a closed polygon must total n × 360° − (n − 2) × 180° = (n + 2) × 180°. And for a traverse turned by deflection angles, the algebraic sum — counting right deflections as positive and left deflections as negative — of a closed loop equals exactly 360° (the total change in direction in going once around).

Each condition does the same job in a different language: it provides a fixed total that correct field angles must reproduce, so the discrepancy is the misclosure. A surveyor chooses the angle convention that fits the job, then applies the matching geometric condition as the closure check. The condition for interior angles, (n − 2)×180°, is the one you will use most.

rows: Reduce to interior, then (n - 2) x 180 deg

content.text: Worked check: a five-sided (n = 5) closed traverse should have interior angles summing to (5 - 2) x 180 = 540 deg. If the five measured angles total 540 deg 02 min 30 sec, the angular misclosure is +02 min 30 sec (150 sec) — small, and within tolerance for a typical traverse, so the next lesson distributes it. A total of 545 deg would betray a roughly 5-degree blunder to find.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Interior angles are inside a closed figure, exterior angles outside, and deflection angles are measured from the prolongation of the previous line (R or L).
Closed polygon interior angles must sum to (n - 2) x 180 deg; this is an exact geometric theorem.
Exterior angles sum to (n + 2) x 180 deg; deflection angles (R positive, L negative) sum to 360 deg around a loop.
Comparing the measured angle total to the required sum gives the angular misclosure — an independent blunder check.

flashcards.definition: An angle measured on the inside of a closed figure between two adjacent traverse lines. For a closed n-sided polygon the interior angles must sum to (n - 2) x 180 degrees.

An angle measured from the prolongation (straight-ahead extension) of the previous line to the next line, labeled right (R) or left (L). Common on open route traverses; the algebraic sum around a closed loop is 360 degrees.
The geometric requirement that the interior angles of a closed n-sided polygon total exactly (n - 2) x 180 degrees, used as an independent check on measured traverse angles.
The difference between the measured sum of a closed traverse's angles and the geometrically required sum. A small value indicates good angle work; a large value indicates a blunder.

lessons.title: Angle Balancing and Adjustment
learning_objectives: Compute the angular misclosure of a closed traverse as the measured sum minus the required geometric sum.
Decide whether a misclosure is acceptable by comparing it to an allowable based on instrument and number of angles.
Balance (adjust) the angles by distributing the misclosure, normally equally, as a correction of -(misclosure)/n per angle.
Explain why angles are balanced before directions and coordinates are computed, and how to handle a non-evenly-divisible misclosure.
key_takeaways: Angular misclosure = (sum of measured interior angles) - (n - 2)*180 deg.
If the misclosure is within the allowable, distribute it; the per-angle correction is -(misclosure)/n.
Corrections are applied with the opposite sign of the misclosure so the adjusted angles total exactly (n-2)*180 deg.
Angles are balanced first because every subsequent bearing/azimuth and coordinate depends on them.

content.html:
From Misclosure to Adjustment

The previous lesson showed that a closed traverse's measured angles rarely total the exact geometric requirement; the small leftover is the angular misclosure. Adjustment — also called balancing the angles — is the step that distributes that misclosure back into the individual angles so the set becomes geometrically consistent before any direction or coordinate is computed. It is the first adjustment performed in reducing a traverse, and everything downstream depends on it.

The logic is the error theory from the measurements module made concrete. Once any blunder has been ruled out (a large misclosure means a mistake, not noise), the remaining small misclosure is treated as accumulated random error spread across the angles. Because random errors are sign-balanced and we have no reason to blame one station over another, we distribute the correction — usually equally — among all the angles.

Computing and Judging the Misclosure

For interior angles the misclosure is computed directly:

  • Misclosure = (sum of measured interior angles) − (n − 2) × 180°.

A positive misclosure means the angles totaled too much; a negative one, too little. Before distributing it you must decide whether it is acceptable. A common rule of thumb sets the allowable angular misclosure proportional to the instrument's least count and the number of angles — for example, on the order of k·√n, where k reflects the instrument grade (a finer instrument allows a tighter total). If the misclosure exceeds the allowable, you do not adjust; you return to the field, because an out-of-tolerance misclosure signals a blunder or a procedural problem that adjustment would only smear across good angles.

content.label: Angular misclosure and equal per-angle correction c

content.html:
Balancing the Angles

Once the misclosure passes the tolerance test, balancing is simple. The standard method distributes it equally: the correction applied to each of the n angles is

  • correction per angle = −(misclosure) / n.

The minus sign matters: the correction has the opposite sign of the misclosure, so adding it pulls the total back to the exact geometric value. If a five-angle traverse has a misclosure of +2′30″ (150″ over), each angle is reduced by 150″ / 5 = 30″, and the adjusted angles now total exactly 540°00′00″. Always confirm the adjusted angles re-sum to (n − 2)×180° as a closing check.

When the misclosure does not divide evenly into seconds, you apply slightly larger corrections to a few angles — conventionally those observed under poorer conditions (short sights, where pointing error is largest) — so the corrections still sum to the full misclosure. For rigorous control work, least-squares adjustment (introduced in the measurements module) replaces this equal-distribution rule, weighting each angle by its reliability; equal distribution is the appropriate, defensible choice for ordinary traverses.

content.text: Balance the ANGLES before computing bearings, azimuths, latitudes, or departures. Every direction in the traverse is carried from the previous one by adding a measured angle, so an unbalanced angular error propagates into every subsequent line. Adjusting coordinates without first balancing angles bakes the angular error into the position of every station.

content.html:
Order of Operations

Why must angle balancing come first? Because direction is carried forward: the azimuth of each new line equals the azimuth of the previous line plus the measured angle at the station. An error in one angle therefore shifts the direction of that line and every line after it. If you computed bearings from raw, unbalanced angles, the angular misclosure would reappear as a direction error and then as a linear misclosure in the coordinates — mixing two separate problems together.

The clean workflow is sequential: (1) turn and mean the angles, (2) compute the angular misclosure and check it against tolerance, (3) balance the angles equally, (4) carry adjusted azimuths/bearings around the traverse, (5) compute latitudes and departures and then perform the linear (coordinate) adjustment. Each step rests on the consistency established by the one before it, and angle balancing is the foundation of the whole sequence.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Angular misclosure = sum of measured interior angles minus (n - 2) x 180 deg.
Compare the misclosure to an allowable (roughly k*sqrt(n)); if it exceeds it, re-observe rather than adjust.
Balance equally: each angle gets a correction of -(misclosure)/n, opposite in sign to the misclosure.
Balance angles before computing directions and coordinates, because direction is carried forward from angle to angle.

flashcards.definition: Distributing the angular misclosure of a closed traverse back into the measured angles so they satisfy the geometric sum condition exactly, performed before directions and coordinates are computed.
flashcards.hint_1: Spread the misclosure across the angles.

flashcards.definition: For equal distribution, the correction applied to each angle is -(misclosure)/n: the misclosure divided by the number of angles, with opposite sign so the adjusted angles total the required geometric sum.
The maximum misclosure a traverse may have and still be adjusted rather than re-observed, set roughly proportional to the instrument's least count and the square root of the number of angles.

lessons.title: Direction Computation Exercises
learning_objectives: Convert azimuths to quadrant bearings and bearings to azimuths in all four quadrants.
Compute back-azimuths and carry direction from one traverse line to the next using a measured angle.
Compute the angular misclosure of an n-sided traverse and the resulting per-angle correction.
Determine the azimuth of a line from the interior angle between two known azimuths.
key_takeaways: Work direction problems by first identifying the quadrant, then applying the matching conversion formula.
Carry azimuths forward: next azimuth = back-azimuth of the previous line + interior angle (reduce modulo 360 deg).
Angular misclosure of an n-sided closed traverse = measured sum - (n - 2)*180 deg; per-angle correction = -misclosure/n.
The interior angle between two lines equals the difference of their azimuths (taken as the value between 0 and 360 deg, or its 360-complement).

content.html:
Putting Directions to Work

This closing lesson is deliberately computational: it ties together azimuths, bearings, back-directions, angle sums, and balancing into the actual arithmetic a surveyor performs to reduce field angles into directions. None of it introduces new theory — it applies the conversions and conditions from the previous lessons to worked examples and to the randomized practice problems attached to this module. Master the routine here and traverse computation becomes mechanical.

The single most valuable habit is to sketch first. A quick plan-view diagram with north up, the line drawn in its approximate direction, and the relevant angle marked, resolves nearly every quadrant and sign question before you touch the formulas. Surveyors who skip the sketch are the ones who put a southeast line in the northeast quadrant.

Worked Example: Azimuth to Bearing

Convert an azimuth of 208°30′ to a quadrant bearing. First identify the quadrant: 208° lies between 180° and 270°, the southwest quadrant. The southwest rule is bearing angle = azimuth − 180°, so 208°30′ − 180° = 28°30′, and the bearing is S 28°30′ W.

Reverse it as a check: a southwest bearing converts back with azimuth = 180° + bearing angle = 180° + 28°30′ = 208°30′. The round trip recovers the original azimuth, confirming the conversion. Try a northwest case too: an azimuth of 318°15′ is in the NW quadrant (270–360°), so the bearing angle is 360° − 318°15′ = 41°45′, giving N 41°45′ W.

Worked Example: Carrying Azimuth Around a Traverse

Suppose line AB has a known azimuth of 62°10′, and at station B you measure the interior angle ABC (angle to the right) as 115°40′. To get the azimuth of BC, first reverse AB to get the direction looking back from B to A — the back-azimuth of AB = 62°10′ + 180° = 242°10′. Then add the measured angle to swing from that backsight direction to the foresight:

  • Azimuth BC = back-azimuth of AB + angle ABC = 242°10′ + 115°40′ = 357°50′.

Since the result is under 360°, no reduction is needed; the azimuth of BC is 357°50′. Had the sum exceeded 360°, you would subtract 360°. This back-azimuth-plus-angle step, repeated station by station, is exactly how direction is carried around an entire traverse — which is why the angles must be balanced first.

content.label: Carrying azimuth forward with interior angle theta (angle to the right)

content.html:
Worked Example: Misclosure and Correction

A six-sided (n = 6) closed traverse should have interior angles summing to (6 − 2) × 180° = 720°00′00″. Say the six measured angles total 720°01′48″. The angular misclosure is 720°01′48″ − 720°00′00″ = +01′48″, i.e. +108″. Comparing to a typical allowable for six angles, this is small and acceptable.

Balance it equally: the per-angle correction is −(misclosure)/n = −108″ / 6 = −18″ per angle. Subtracting 18″ from each of the six measured angles removes the full 108″ surplus, and the adjusted angles now total exactly 720°00′00″. Always re-add the corrected angles as a final check — they must reproduce (n − 2)×180° precisely.

Interior Angle From Two Azimuths

The inverse problem appears constantly: given the azimuths of two lines radiating from a station, find the angle between them. The interior angle is the difference of the two azimuths. If line 1 has azimuth Az1 and line 2 has azimuth Az2, then the angle from line 1 clockwise to line 2 is (Az2 − Az1), adding 360° if the result is negative. The angle the other way around is its 360° complement.

For example, with Az1 = 40°00′ and Az2 = 155°30′, the clockwise angle from line 1 to line 2 is 155°30′ − 40°00′ = 115°30′; the reflex angle the other way is 360° − 115°30′ = 244°30′. Deciding which one is the "interior" angle of your figure is, once again, settled by a quick sketch. These four routines — convert, reverse, carry, and difference — are the entire toolkit of direction computation.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Identify the quadrant first, then convert: SW azimuth - 180, NW 360 - azimuth, and so on; check by converting back.
Carry direction forward: next azimuth = (previous azimuth + 180 deg + interior angle) reduced modulo 360 deg.
Misclosure of an n-sided closed traverse = measured sum - (n - 2) x 180 deg; equal correction = -misclosure/n.
The angle between two lines is the difference of their azimuths (add 360 deg if negative; the other angle is the 360-complement).

flashcards.definition: Computing the azimuth of the next traverse line as the back-azimuth of the previous line plus the measured interior angle, reduced modulo 360 degrees. The mechanism by which direction is propagated around a traverse.
flashcards.hint_1: Back-azimuth of previous line plus the angle.

flashcards.definition: The angle between two lines from a common station, equal to the difference of their azimuths (Az2 - Az1, adding 360 degrees if negative). The angle the other way is the 360-degree complement.

The habit of identifying which compass quadrant a direction lies in (from the azimuth's range or the bearing's letters) before applying the matching azimuth-bearing conversion formula, preventing sign and quadrant errors.

quiz_questions.question_text: A horizontal angle measured clockwise from the backsight to the foresight is called:
quiz_questions.explanation: An angle to the right is turned clockwise from backsight to foresight and is the modern default for traverse work. A deflection angle is measured from the prolongation of the previous line, and a zenith angle is a vertical-plane angle.

quiz_questions.question_text: Observing an angle in both the direct and reverse (face left and face right) positions and meaning the results primarily serves to:
options: Reduce random pointing error by averaging
Cancel systematic instrument errors such as collimation and trunnion-axis error
Convert the angle from a bearing to an azimuth
Increase the measured angle by a factor of two
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Cancel systematic instrument errors such as collimation and trunnion-axis error
quiz_questions.explanation: These instrument errors reverse sign between faces, so meaning the two faces cancels them. Random error is what additional sets average down; double-centering targets the systematic instrument errors.

quiz_questions.question_text: A horizontal angle depends on the difference in elevation between the backsight and foresight targets.
quiz_questions.explanation: A horizontal angle is measured in a horizontal plane and is independent of the targets' elevations; the instrument reads only the projection of the directions onto a level plane.

quiz_questions.question_text: A total station reads a zenith angle of 87 deg 00 min to a target. What is the equivalent vertical (altitude) angle?
quiz_questions.explanation: Vertical angle V = 90 deg - Z = 90 deg - 87 deg = +3 deg. A zenith angle less than 90 deg is a sight above horizontal, so the vertical angle is positive.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement about zenith and vertical angles is correct?
options: A zenith angle is measured up or down from the horizontal.
A level line of sight is 0 deg zenith and 90 deg vertical.
A level line of sight is 90 deg zenith and 0 deg vertical.
A zenith angle can be negative below horizontal.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A level line of sight is 90 deg zenith and 0 deg vertical.
quiz_questions.explanation: Zenith angles are measured from straight up (0 deg at zenith, 90 deg at horizontal, always positive). A level sight is therefore 90 deg zenith and 0 deg vertical.

quiz_questions.question_text: Observing both instrument faces removes the vertical-circle index error.
quiz_questions.explanation: The index error enters with opposite sign in the two faces, so reducing the direct and reverse readings together removes it. For zenith angles, the two faces should sum to 360 deg.

quiz_questions.question_text: An azimuth of 145 deg 00 min expressed as a quadrant bearing is:
quiz_questions.explanation: 145 deg lies in the SE quadrant (90 to 180 deg), so the bearing angle is 180 - 145 = 35 deg and the line points south-and-east: S 35 deg 00 min E.

quiz_questions.question_text: The bearing S 50 deg 00 min W converted to an azimuth is:
quiz_questions.explanation: A southwest bearing converts with azimuth = 180 + bearing angle = 180 + 50 = 230 deg.

quiz_questions.question_text: A line has a forward azimuth of 70 deg. Its back-azimuth is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Back-azimuth = forward azimuth +/- 180 deg. Since 70 deg < 180 deg, add 180: 70 + 180 = 250 deg.

quiz_questions.question_text: The back-bearing of N 35 deg E is S 35 deg W.
quiz_questions.explanation: A back-bearing keeps the same numeric angle and flips both letters: N becomes S and E becomes W, giving S 35 deg W.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under modern Texas practice, what is the typical basis of bearing that must be referenced in a survey description?
options: Magnetic north as read on the day of survey
Grid north on the Texas State Plane Coordinate System (NAD83)
An arbitrary assumed north with no reference
True north determined by solar observation only
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Grid north on the Texas State Plane Coordinate System (NAD83)
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas rules (22 TAC 138.91(c)) require a stated basis of bearing; modern practice ties to grid north on the Texas State Plane Coordinate System (NAD83). Magnetic/assumed bearings appear in historic deeds and must be reconciled to this datum.

quiz_questions.question_text: Magnetic declination is best defined as:
options: The angle between grid north and true north
The horizontal angle between true north and magnetic north at a place
The vertical angle of the earth's magnetic field
The error caused by nearby iron near the instrument
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The horizontal angle between true north and magnetic north at a place
quiz_questions.explanation: Declination (magnetic variation) is the horizontal angle between true north and the direction the compass needle points (magnetic north). The grid-to-true angle is convergence, and nearby-iron deflection is local attraction.

quiz_questions.question_text: A magnetic bearing of N 20 deg 00 min E is observed where the declination is 8 deg 00 min EAST. The true bearing is:
quiz_questions.explanation: True = magnetic + east declination = 20 deg + 8 deg = 28 deg, so N 28 deg 00 min E. An east declination is added to the magnetic bearing.

quiz_questions.question_text: When retracing an old Texas survey recorded in magnetic bearings, you should use today's declination because the magnetic field does not change.
quiz_questions.explanation: Declination undergoes secular change over time, so you must use the declination of the original survey's date and place, not today's. Recovered monuments control over the magnetic course in any case.

quiz_questions.question_text: When a line's forward and back compass bearings differ by an amount other than exactly 180 deg, the most likely cause is:
options: Local attraction at one or both stations
An error in the (n - 2) x 180 deg angle sum
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Local attraction at one or both stations
quiz_questions.explanation: Forward and back bearings of a line should be true reciprocals (180 deg apart). A departure from 180 deg indicates local attraction deflecting the needle at one or both ends.

quiz_questions.question_text: The interior angles of a closed seven-sided (heptagon) traverse must sum to:
quiz_questions.explanation: Sum of interior angles = (n - 2) x 180 deg = (7 - 2) x 180 = 5 x 180 = 900 deg.

quiz_questions.question_text: A deflection angle is measured:
options: Inside a closed figure between the two adjacent lines
From the prolongation of the previous line to the next line, labeled right or left
Clockwise from north to the line
quiz_questions.correct_answer: From the prolongation of the previous line to the next line, labeled right or left
quiz_questions.explanation: A deflection angle is measured from the straight-ahead extension (prolongation) of the incoming line to the outgoing line, designated R or L. It is common on open route traverses.

quiz_questions.question_text: The algebraic sum of the deflection angles (right positive, left negative) around a closed traverse loop equals 360 deg.
quiz_questions.explanation: Going once around a closed loop the direction changes by a full 360 deg, so the deflection angles (R positive, L negative) sum algebraically to 360 deg.

quiz_questions.question_text: A closed five-sided traverse has measured interior angles summing to 540 deg 01 min 40 sec. The angular misclosure is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Required sum = (5 - 2) x 180 = 540 deg. Misclosure = 540 deg 01 min 40 sec - 540 deg = +01 min 40 sec (the measured total exceeds the geometric requirement).

quiz_questions.question_text: If the angular misclosure of a six-angle traverse is +120 sec, the equal correction applied to each angle is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Per-angle correction = -(misclosure)/n = -120/6 = -20 sec. The correction is opposite in sign to the misclosure so the adjusted angles total exactly the required sum.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why must the angles of a traverse be balanced before bearings and coordinates are computed?
options: Because the linear misclosure is always larger than the angular misclosure
Because direction is carried forward from angle to angle, so an angular error propagates into every later line
Because coordinates cannot be computed from azimuths
Because balancing changes the measured distances
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Because direction is carried forward from angle to angle, so an angular error propagates into every later line
quiz_questions.explanation: Each line's azimuth is computed from the previous azimuth plus a measured angle, so an unbalanced angular error shifts the direction of that line and all following lines, corrupting every subsequent coordinate.

quiz_questions.question_text: Line AB has azimuth 50 deg 00 min. At station B the interior angle ABC (angle to the right) is 120 deg 00 min. The azimuth of line BC is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Azimuth BC = back-azimuth of AB + angle = (50 + 180) + 120 = 230 + 120 = 350 deg. The result is under 360 deg, so no reduction is needed.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two lines from a station have azimuths 40 deg 00 min and 155 deg 30 min. The clockwise angle from the first line to the second is:
quiz_questions.explanation: The angle is the difference of azimuths: 155 deg 30 min - 40 deg 00 min = 115 deg 30 min. The reflex angle the other way is 360 - 115 deg 30 min = 244 deg 30 min.

quiz_questions.question_text: Convert the azimuth 318 deg 15 min to a quadrant bearing.
quiz_questions.explanation: 318 deg lies in the NW quadrant (270 to 360 deg), so the bearing angle is 360 - 318 deg 15 min = 41 deg 45 min, giving N 41 deg 45 min W.

problem_templates.name: Convert azimuth to quadrant bearing
problem_templates.category: Angle and Direction Measurement — Bearings and Azimuths
problem_templates.question_template: A line has an azimuth of {{az:dms}} (measured clockwise from north). The line falls in the southwest quadrant. Compute the BEARING ANGLE (the acute angle from south toward west), in decimal degrees.

solution_steps_template.title: Identify the quadrant
solution_steps_template.calculation_template: {{az:f2}} deg is between 180 and 270 deg -> SW quadrant

problem_templates.explanation_template: For an azimuth in the SW quadrant (180 to 270 deg), the bearing is S (azimuth - 180) W. Here {{az:f2}} - 180 = {{_answer}} deg, so the bearing is S {{_answer}} deg W.

problem_templates.name: Convert bearing to azimuth
problem_templates.category: Angle and Direction Measurement — Bearings and Azimuths
problem_templates.question_template: A line has the quadrant bearing S {{b:dms}} E (south-and-east). Compute the AZIMUTH of the line (clockwise from north), in decimal degrees.

solution_steps_template.title: Identify the quadrant
solution_steps_template.calculation_template: S {{b:f2}} E -> SE quadrant (azimuth 90 to 180 deg)

problem_templates.explanation_template: A southeast bearing converts to azimuth with azimuth = 180 - bearing angle = 180 - {{b:f2}} = {{_answer}} deg.

problem_templates.name: Angular misclosure and per-angle correction
problem_templates.category: Angle and Direction Measurement — Angle Balancing
problem_templates.question_template: A closed traverse has {{n}} sides. The sum of the measured interior angles exceeds the geometric requirement by a misclosure of {{misc}} seconds. Using equal distribution, compute the CORRECTION applied to each angle, in seconds (report the magnitude).

solution_steps_template.title: Per-angle correction
solution_steps_template.result_template: {{misc}} / {{n}} = {{_answer}} seconds (applied as -{{_answer}} sec to each angle)

problem_templates.explanation_template: The interior angles should sum to (n - 2) x 180 = {{required}} deg. The +{{misc}} sec misclosure is distributed equally: each of the {{n}} angles is corrected by misclosure/n = {{misc}}/{{n}} = {{_answer}} seconds (negative, opposite the misclosure).

problem_templates.name: Interior angle from two azimuths
problem_templates.category: Angle and Direction Measurement — Direction Computation
problem_templates.question_template: From a single station, line 1 has azimuth {{az1:dms}} and line 2 has azimuth {{az2:dms}} (with az2 greater than az1). Compute the clockwise ANGLE from line 1 to line 2, in decimal degrees.
problem_templates.explanation_template: The angle between two lines from a station is the difference of their azimuths: {{az2:f2}} - {{az1:f2}} = {{_answer}} deg. (The reflex angle the other way is 360 - {{_answer}} deg.)
